questions.question: An interviewer asks: 'If you override equals on a class without overriding hashCode, what specifically breaks?' What's the cleanest answer?
choices: The compiler refuses to compile the class.
HashMap/HashSet lookups become unreliable: HashMap routes by hashCode first, so equal-but-different-hash objects land in different buckets - contains/get will fail to find the equal entry.
Performance degrades because Java falls back to a slow O(n) scan.
questions.explanation: The contract is: equal objects must have equal hashCodes. If equals says two objects are equal but hashCode disagrees, HashMap routes them to different buckets and the equal-object lookup silently fails. The collection is corrupted; nothing else fails.

questions.question: An interview question asks why LinkedList is rarely the right choice over ArrayList in modern Java. Best framing?
choices: ArrayList has better cache locality, lower per-element memory overhead, and faster random access. LinkedList's O(1) middle-insertion advantage only materialises if you already hold the node - which you almost never do in practice.
LinkedList is single-threaded; ArrayList is concurrent.
questions.explanation: ArrayList is contiguous memory; CPU caches love it. LinkedList allocates a node per element with two pointers each, blowing cache and adding GC pressure. The textbook O(1) insert benefit requires holding the node - searching for an insert point is O(n), and then ArrayList's bulk shift is faster than chasing pointers.

questions.question: What's the canonical Java 17+ idiom for modelling a discriminated union of payment outcomes (Success, Declined, Pending)?
choices: Three classes extending an abstract Payment, plus an enum field for the type.
A sealed interface Payment permits Success, Declined, Pending - with each variant as a record. Then handle with an exhaustive pattern-matching switch expression.
A single class with three boolean flags and nullable fields.
An enum with three constants and a payload Object.
questions.explanation: Sealed interface + record subtypes + pattern-matching switch is the modern Java 17+ idiom for discriminated unions. The compiler enforces exhaustiveness - adding a new permitted subtype breaks every switch at compile time, removing the runtime surprise.
